detailed_analysis: mcp_server_io.py is a 612-line dialect-aware JSON marshalling layer for MCP server slots. It is competently structured into private helpers, but the surface has accumulated several structural smells. The two public entry points, parse_mcp_server_json (66 lines) and render_mcp_server_json, are at the edge of the Long Function limit and mix four levels of abstraction in one body (orchestration, dialect dispatch, secret-policy invocation, dict surgery). The whole module is built around an implicit dict schema: 'canonical', 'obj', 'tool_only', 'prior_obj' all flow through helpers as raw dict[str, Any], so type signatures convey nothing about which keys are required and which are optional. This is textbook Primitive Obsession (W8) — a CanonicalMcpServer dataclass and a SlotDocument wrapper would make the contract checkable and would localise the schema in one place instead of in _clear_mcp_fields, _known_slot_fields, _copy_transport_fields, _render_transport_fields, _tool_only_spellings, and the field-name strings scattered across the file. The same primitive-obsession appears in tool_only, where field names are accessed by hardcoded string keys ('transport_field', 'url_field', 'env_field', 'disabled_field', etc.) duplicated between writer (_tool_only_spellings) and reader (_render_*); any rename is an unbounded grep-and-pray. The dialect itself is a partial God-Class (M1): 18 fields, several of which describe both 'how to parse' and 'how to render', plus a method canonical_transport — and the dialect is then accessed both directly and indirectly through tool_only overrides, which means the rendering policy lives in three places (DEFAULT dialect, tool_only spellings, prior_obj sniffing in _render_field_name). The transport-fork pattern (if transport == 'stdio': ... else: ...) is repeated in _copy_transport_fields, _render_transport_fields, and implicitly in _transport_from_slot — a Divergent Change risk (M8): adding a fourth transport requires editing three switch-like blocks plus _known_slot_fields plus _clear_mcp_fields. Security-wise the module delegates secret handling to mcp_secret_policy, which is the correct move; however parse_mcp_server_json silently leaks the original obj through 'per_agentic_tool_extra' (lines 148-153) AFTER apply_mcp_secret_policy returned a redacted obj — so any secret-bearing key that is NOT in _known_slot_fields (e.g. an unknown vendor field that holds a literal token) will be copied verbatim into the canonical doc and persisted to state. The policy module only rewrites known field paths; the catch-all 'extras' is a back-door around the policy. The most concerning correctness smell is _loads_slot's silent fallback from JSON to TOML (lines 206-209): a slot named mcp.json that happens to be syntactically valid TOML (which is very easy — TOML is permissive about lines like 'name = "x"') will silently parse as TOML, producing keys with different shapes. The function name says 'json'; the call sites expect JSON; the test suite never exercises TOML. Similarly extract_pair_id_from_mcp_server_json catches TOMLDecodeError + JSONDecodeError + ValueError and returns None (C3) — three different failure modes squashed into 'no pair_id'. The test file is short and reasonably tight but: (a) the round-trip test computes 'rendered' and 'parsed' but never asserts that rendered == text or parsed == canonical; (b) tests cover stdio and http aliases but never sse / streamable-http despite both being in CANONICAL_TRANSPORTS; (c) secret-policy parametrised test only asserts that field_name appears in findings; (d) no test for the TOML fallback, unknown transport, prior_canonical reuse, bearer_token_env_var_field round-trip, or env_http_headers_field path. Verdict: AUDIT_WEAK.

findings.problem: apply_mcp_secret_policy only inspects fields it knows about. The 'extras' dict then captures every unknown key verbatim. Any vendor-specific field whose path is not in the classifier (e.g. 'vendor_flag', 'authentication_blob', 'license_key') will pass through into canonical state with literal secrets. The extras pathway is an unaudited persistence channel.
On a JSONDecodeError the loader silently retries with tomllib.loads. The dual-format fallback is neither documented nor exercised by any test.

Three distinct failure modes — malformed JSON, malformed TOML, slot-not-an-object — are collapsed into 'no pair_id'. Callers cannot distinguish 'this slot has no pair_id (new artifact)' from 'this slot is corrupt and should not be touched'.

Single dataclass mixes four distinct responsibilities: (1) recognized-field aliases for parsing, (2) preferred field-name on rendering, (3) value-shape policy, (4) optional behavioural fields tied to security flows. SRP violation.

findings.variant: Long Function with mixed levels of abstraction
findings.problem: 66 lines, 11 branching points, 6 distinct responsibilities. Note the dead-parameter pattern at line 102: del artifact_root.
artifact_root is in the public signature but immediately discarded.

Adding a new transport requires coordinated edits in at least 5 locations. The current 'stdio else http' fork already conflates sse, http, and streamable-http into one branch despite being three transports in CANONICAL_TRANSPORTS.
The tool_only dict uses nine magic string keys that the writer and reader must agree on by convention. A typo is a silent runtime bug — no schema, no enum, no dataclass.

50-line function with three sequential header-extraction phases. Nesting reaches 4 levels. The bearer block and env_http_headers block share the same shape but are coded twice. This is also the only place where header secrets are unmasked back into env_var fields — a security-relevant transform with no test that exercises bearer_token_env_var_field or env_http_headers_field.

findings.variant: Inappropriate Intimacy with canonical dict schema
findings.problem: Six modules consume the 'canonical' dict shape with hardcoded string keys. There is no CanonicalMcpServer dataclass, so the schema is enforced only by the union of all callers' string accesses.
The aliases dict is rebuilt from self.transport_aliases on every call.

_known_slot_fields and _clear_mcp_fields drift independently: adding a new field requires updating both, plus _copy_transport_fields, _render_transport_fields, and the dialect's *_fields tuples. Five places per concept.
apply_mcp_secret_policy returns (obj, redactions) and the parser uses both — but the renderer discards 'redactions' with '_'. The fact that a redaction happened is silently lost.

The test name promises a round-trip but the assertions check 'canonical' fields plus a single key on 'parsed'. 'rendered' is computed and never asserted: no check that json.loads(rendered) == original_dict, no check that parsed == canonical (the actual round-trip invariant).
Five parametrised slots. The single assertion only checks that 'field_name in findings' — it does NOT check that exactly that finding fired or that no false positives appeared.

CANONICAL_TRANSPORTS declares 4 transports; tests exercise 2. bearer_token_env_var_field and env_http_headers_field are untested. _loads_slot's JSON->TOML fallback has no test. command_mode='array' is unexercised. _render_transport_value's fallback to transport_render_values is unexercised.

verdict_summary: No CRITICAL security defect lands, but the module has clear Divergent Change across transport branches, Primitive Obsession on a canonical-dict schema with 9 stringly-typed tool_only keys, a silent JSON-to-TOML fallback in _loads_slot, and an extras-back-door past the secret policy; the test file under-asserts and leaves 7 declared behaviours unexercised.
